coredata.dc:description:
The tumor microenvironment (TME) exhibits a specific feature of hypoxia, which poses significant challenges for oxygen (O2)-dependent treatments. In this study, we developed an intelligent nanoplatform (PEGylated AIPH@MSN/CDs-MnO2, denoted as A@M/C-Mn) by integrating a photosensitizer of red carbon dots (CDs) with a thermolabile initiator-loaded mesoporous silica nanoparticle (AIPH@MSN, denoted as A@M), and then growing manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NS) in situ and PEGylating the structure to achieve TME-responsive synergistic diagnosis and phototherapy against hypoxic tumors. The outer-layer MnO2 NS has the capability to decompose endogenous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the acidic TME, thereby producing O2 to alleviate hypoxia while releasing Mn2+. This process restores the fluorescence (FL) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) properties of the CDs, enhancing singlet oxygen (1O2) generation upon near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation. Concomitantly, the exposed CDs induce hyperthermia for photothermal therapy (PTT) and promote the decomposition of AIPH to form cytotoxic alkyl radicals (
R) for O2-independent PDT. Importantly, the entire treatment process can be monitored through ultrasound (US)/magnetic resonance (MR)/photoacoustic (PA)/FL imaging, owing to O2 production, Mn2+ release, and CDs activation, respectively. Both in vitro and in vivo results provide evidence that A@M/C-Mn represents a promising theranostic nanoagent for hypoxic tumors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of A@M/C-Mn preparation and its applications for multi-modal imaging and treatment.

TEM images of (A) CDs, (B) A@M, (C) A@M/C, and (D) A@M/C-Mn (inset: high resolution). (E) EDS spectrum of A@M/C-Mn. (F-G) HAADF/EDS-STEM images of A@M/C-Mn and corresponding element mapping of Mn, O, Si and N (yellow block: scan area of mapping). (H) FTIR spectra of AIPH, MSN, CDs, A@M/C, and A@M/C-Mn. (I) UV–Vis absorption of CDs, MSN, A@M, A@M/C, and A@M/C-Mn aqueous dispersions. (J) FL emission spectra of DI H2O, A@M/C, and A@M/C-Mn aqueous dispersions (excitation at 420 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Photothermal curves of A@M/C-Mn at various concentrations under 650 nm laser irradiation (1 W cm−2). (B) Photothermal curves of A@M/C-Mn (100 μg mL−1) exposed to 650 nm laser with various power densities. (C) Corresponding IR images of (A). (D) Photothermal curves of A@M/C-Mn for four on/off cycles of laser irradiation at 1 W cm−2. (E) Photographs of O2 generation from A@M/C-Mn in acidic H2O2 (pH 6.5) at 1 min and 5 min. (F) O2 generation from various concentrations of A@M/C-Mn in H2O2 at pH 6.5. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (G-I) TEM images of A@M/C-Mn after incubation in H2O2 at pH 6.5 for 0, 2, and 8 h, respectively.

(A) O2 generation by A@M/C-Mn in H2O2 solutions with different pH values. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (B) Detection of generated
R from AIPH by ABTS with prolonged time under different temperatures. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (C) Detection of generated ABTS+ at 37 °C in normoxic and hypoxic conditions. (D) Detection of generated
R from AIPH, M/C-Mn and A@M/C-Mn under 650 nm laser irradiation (1 W cm−2). Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (E) Laser-induced ABTS+ generation from A@M/C-Mn at different time points. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (F) Cumulative release of AIPH from A@M/C-Mn at different pH values at 37 °C. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (G) Normalized FL intensity of SOSG at 525 nm after incubation with different samples in the presence of H2O2 under 650 nm laser irradiation (0.15 W cm−2, pH 6.5). Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (H) FL spectra of SOSG in solutions containing A@M/C-Mn under 650 nm laser irradiation (0.15 W cm−2) over time in a normoxic condition. (I) FL recovery of CDs from A@M/C-Mn at different pH values. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment.

(A) FL images of 4T1 cells incubated with CDs, A@M/C, and A@M/C-Mn for 4 h. (B) Intracellular free radicals detection by DCFH-DA under different laser irradiation in normoxia (L0.15: 0.15 W cm−2, L1: 1 W cm−2). Scale bar = 10 μm. (C) Cell viability of 4T1 cells after different treated in normoxia. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. Statistical analysis was carried out by one-way ANOVA test and statistical significance was set at *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. (D) The live/dead co-staining for live/dead cell differentiation after different treated in normoxia. Scale bar = 20 μm.

(A) In vivo FL images of tumor models after i.v. injection of A@M/C-Mn for 0, 1, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 24 h. (B) Corresponding average FL values at tumor sites. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (C) Average PA values of A@M/C-Mn at different concentration (inset: corresponding PA images in test tubes. (D) In vivo US/PA/sO2 images of tumor sites after i.v. injection of A@M/C-Mn for 0, 1, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 24 h. (E-G) Corresponding average US/PA/sO2 values at tumor sites. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (H) Linear relationship between 1/T1 and Mn content (inset: T1-weighted MR images of A@M/C-Mn with different Mn content (pH 7.4 and pH 6.5). (I) In vivo T1-weighted MR images of tumor sites after i.v. injection of A@M/C-Mn for 0, 1, 3, 6, and 9 h. (J) Corresponding average MR signal values at tumor sites. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment.

(A) IR images of tumor site under laser irradiation for 10 min after 6 h post-injection. (B) Corresponding temperature of tumor site at irradiation time point of 10 min (n = 3). Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (C) Body weight, (D) Tumor volume, and (E) Tumor weight of mice after different treatments. Data shown as mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. Statistical analysis was carried out by one-way ANOVA test and statistical significance was set at *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. (F) Digital images of tumors and mice in each group after 14 days of treatment. (G) TUNEL (scale bar = 10 μm) and (H) H&E (scale bar = 100 μm) staining of tumor sections after different treatments.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel tumor microenvironment-activated and near-infrared light-driven free radicals amplifier (A@M/C-Mn) was constructed for tetra-modal ultrasound/magnetic resonance/photoacoustic/fluorescence cancer imaging and synergistic photothermal/O2-dependent/O2-independent treatment.

The tumor microenvironment (TME) exhibits a specific feature of hypoxia, which poses significant challenges for oxygen (O2)-dependent treatments. In this study, we developed an intelligent nanoplatform (PEGylated AIPH@MSN/CDs-MnO2, denoted as A@M/C-Mn) by integrating a photosensitizer of red carbon dots (CDs) with a thermolabile initiator-loaded mesoporous silica nanoparticle (AIPH@MSN, denoted as A@M), and then growing manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NS) in situ and PEGylating the structure to achieve TME-responsive synergistic diagnosis and phototherapy against hypoxic tumors. The outer-layer MnO2 NS has the capability to decompose endogenous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the acidic TME, thereby producing O2 to alleviate hypoxia while releasing Mn2+. This process restores the fluorescence (FL) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) properties of the CDs, enhancing singlet oxygen (1O2) generation upon near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation. Concomitantly, the exposed CDs induce hyperthermia for photothermal therapy (PTT) and promote the decomposition of AIPH to form cytotoxic alkyl radicals (
R) for O2-independent PDT. Importantly, the entire treatment process can be monitored through ultrasound (US)/magnetic resonance (MR)/photoacoustic (PA)/FL imaging, owing to O2 production, Mn2+ release, and CDs activation, respectively. Both in vitro and in vivo results provide evidence that A@M/C-Mn represents a promising theranostic nanoagent for hypoxic tumors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The high incidence and mortality of rates of malignant tumors pose a significant threat to human health and survival [1–4]. In recent years, near-infrared (NIR) activated photodynamic therapy (PDT) has emerged as a promising cancer treatment strategy due to its high spatial selectivity, minimal invasion, and reduced side effects [5–7]. However, the efficacy of PDT is limited in solid tumor sites, particularly deep tumor sites and those located far from blood vessels, which are characterized by hypoxia. This hypoxic environment not only promotes tumor progression and metastasis but also hampers the effectiveness of O2-dependent therapies [8–10]. Moreover, reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated during PDT can deplete O2, exacerbating the hypoxic conditions and leading to PDT resistance [7,11,12]. To overcome these challenges, it is crucial to explore therapeutic agents that can either self-supply O2 or exhibit O2-independent behavior upon NIR light stimulation. Such advancements would significantly improve the performance of PDT [13–17].

Due to the catalase-like catalytic properties and pH responsiveness, manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NS) have garnered significant attention in biomedical applications [18–20]. It has been demonstrated that MnO2 NS can catalyze the production of O2 from endogenous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the acidic tumor microenvironment (TME), thereby enhancing O2-dependent PDT in situ and alleviating tumor hypoxia [21–23]. Meanwhile, thermally decomposable 2,2′-Azobis[2-(2-imidazolin-2-yl)propane] dihydrochloride (AIPH), a promising radical initiator independent of O2, has been employed to address the limitations imposed by tumor hypoxia [24–26]. Upon thermal activation, AIPH decomposes to generate cytotoxic alkyl radicals (
R), enabling PDT without the need for O2. To date, numerous nanomaterials with NIR absorption properties have shown promising therapeutic performance in cancer treatment [27–31]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as highly favorable photosensitizers, gaining widespread attention in various biomedical applications due to their dual capabilities for PDT and photothermal therapy (PTT), coupled with excellent biocompatibility and photostability [32–35].

Cancer diagnosis, particularly TME-responsive imaging, has emerged as a highly promising approach in the field of cancer nanotheranostics, offering numerous advantages [36–43]. Unlike conventional diagnostic methods, activatable imaging agents can selectively target and illuminate cancer cells, providing greater specificity and enabling precise visualization of tumor boundaries [44,45]. Leveraging the distinct biochemical and physiological features of the TME, these imaging techniques enable the early detection of cancer lesions. The fluorescence (FL) of CDs is known to be quenched upon combination with MnO2 due to electron transfer, surface interactions, and energy transfer between them [22,46,47]. However, when exposed to the TME, MnO2 can be degraded by factors such as low pH, H2O2, and glutathione (GSH). This disintegration leads to the release of O2/Mn2+ and the FL recovery of CDs, which offers favorable outcomes for ultrasound (US), magnetic resonance (MR), and FL imaging, respectively [48]. Additionally, the potential of CDs as ideal contrast agents for photoacoustic (PA) imaging has also been explored [49–51].

Glutathione (GSH), 2,2′-Azobis[2-(2-imidazolin-2-yl)propane] Dihydrochloride (AIPH, >98 %), and 2,2′-Azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid ammonium salt) (ABTS, 98 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd. Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 98 %), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 99 %), (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES, ≥98 %), formamide (≥99.5 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98 %), and 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA, ≥97 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4, ≥99 %) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol (≥99.9 %) and nitric acid (HNO3, tracemetal grade, 68 %) were purchased from Anaqua Global International Inc., Ltd (Cleveland, OH, USA). Hoechst 33342, LIVE/DEAD™ Cell Imaging Kit, Promega CellTiter 96™ AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay (MTS), fetal bovine serum (FBS), RPMI 1640 medium, Dulbecco′s phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), LysoTracker™ Green DND-26, and Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green (SOSG) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). JC-1 Assay Kit was acquired from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) were purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, UK). Terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labelin (TUNEL) Assay Kit was obtained from Cell Signaling Technology (Danver, MA, USA). All these chemicals in analytical grade were used without further purification. Deionized water (DI H2O) with resistivity of 18.2 ΜΩ was purified by Millipore Milli-Q and used throughout this work.

The absorption spectra were characterized using an ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (ΜLtrospecTM 2100 pro). The zeta potential measurements were carried out with Zeta-Plus Zeta potential Analyzer (Brookhaven Instrument Corp, USA), using the mode of Phase Analysis Light Scattering. An Edinburgh FLS920P analytical instrument (Edinburgh, UK) with a xenon lamp in 450 W steady state was used to measure the photoluminescence (PL). The morphology was observed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2100F) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) system at 200 kV operating voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Nexsa G2 surface analysis system, Thermo fisher, USA) was performed on an Axis Ultra DLD spectrograph with Al/Kα as the source. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, D/MAX-IIIC, Japan) was used for crystal structures examination. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded within the 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumber range on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer. The element contents were analyzed by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES, Thermo Scientific, USA). An ultrasonic cleaner (DAIHAN Scientific, Korea) in 60 Hz was used to promote the dispersion of solution. In vitro FL imaging was captured using a Leica TCS SPE confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica Microsystems, Germany). Flow cytometry measurements were analyzed using a BD Accuri C6 flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, USA). For PA imaging, a Vevo LAZR multi-modality imaging platform (Fujifilm Visualsonics, Canada) with an integrated fiber-optic linear array LZ250 transducer was utilized. T1-weighted relaxivity and MR imaging experiments were conducted on a 3T MR instrument (Bruker, Germany). In vivo FL imaging was performed using an IVIS Lumina Series III pre-clinical in vivo animal imaging system (PerkinElmer, USA).

MSN was synthesized following a previously reported method [52]. Briefly, 480 mL of DI H2O containing 1 g of CTAB and 280 mg of NaOH was mixed and heated to 80 °C under stirring. Subsequently, 5 g of TEOS was added dropwise and the reaction was allowed to proceed for 2 h. The resulting MSN was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, washed several times with DI H2O, and redispersed in 65 mL of methanol under sonication. Then, 2 mL of APTES was added and the mixture was reacted at room temperature for 24 h. The CTAB/NH2-MSN was obtained after washing with methanol. To remove CTAB, the resultant product was refluxed five times in a methanol (84 mL) solution containing 5.2 mL of HCl at 80 °C. The purified NH2-MSN was then thoroughly washed with methanol and DI H2O, and finally dried for further use.

CDs were synthesized using a one-step, modified microwave-assisted method as described in a previous study [53]. Initially, 0.28 g of GSH was dissolved in 8 mL of formamide to form a transparent solution through sonication. This mixture was then transferred into a two-neck round-bottom flask and placed in a microwave chemical reactor equipped with reflow devices for a 3-min reaction with stirring at 160 °C. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the reaction products were collected and diluted twofold with DI H2O (using a 3.5 kDa cut-off membrane) for 4–5 days to remove residual and small molecules. The resulting product was then centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore membrane to remove larger particles. Finally, the obtained CDs were dried under vacuum for further use.

To prepare AIPH-loaded MSN, an aqueous solution of 20 mg mL−1 AIPH (5 mL) was mixed with 2 mg mL−1 of MSN (5 mL) and stirred for 12 h at room temperature. The AIPH@MSN (denoted as A@M) was separated by centrifugation and washed with DI H2O. The supernatant was also collected to calculate the loading efficacy of AIPH. Subsequently, the resulting A@M was stirred with a 10 mL aqueous solution of CDs (1 mg mL−1) for 12 h in the dark. After centrifugation and washing with DI H2O, the AIPH@MSN/CDs (referred to as A@M/C) was collected and redispersed in DI H2O for further use.

For in situ growth of MnO2 NS, a 20 mg mL−1 of KMnO4 solution (100 mg) was added dropwise to 5 mL of A@M/C suspension (1 mg mL−1) under vigorous stirring at room temperature for 8 h. Following centrifugation and washing with DI H2O, 5 mg of mPEG-NH2 aqueous solution was added to the mixture and stirred for an additional 12 h. The obtained A@M/C-Mn was then purified by centrifugation.

To investigate the photothermal effect performance, A@M/C-Mn at varying concentrations (0–200 μg mL−1) were exposed to a 650 nm laser with a power density of 1 W cm−2 for 10 min. The impact of different power densities (0–2 W cm−2) was also evaluated using a 100 μg mL−1 of sample solution for 10 min. Photostability tests were conducted over four cycles, where each cycle involved turning the laser on for 10 min and then off to allow the temperature to return to room temperature. Real-time temperature measurements and thermal images were recorded using an infrared (IR) thermal camera.

The generation of
R radicals was detected using ABTS. The radical cation ABTS+ was formed via the reaction between the generated
R radicals from thermolabile AIPH and ABTS, which exhibited specific absorption ranging from 500 to 900 nm. Initially, the
R generation properties of AIPH at different temperatures were investigated. An aqueous solution of AIPH (2 mg mL−1) was mixed with ABTS (2 mg mL−1) in equal volume and heated at 25, 37, and 45 °C for 10 min, 1, 2, 4, 6, and 8 h in the dark. Additionally, the mixture of AIPH and ABTS was incubated at 37 °C for 2 h under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions to evaluate the impact of O2 on
R production. The absorption spectra were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. To monitor the formation of ABTS+ upon laser irradiation, an equal volume of A@M/C-Mn (2 mg mL−1) and ABTS (2 mg mL−1) aqueous solutions was mixed and irradiated with a 650 nm laser at a power density of 1 W cm−2. The absorbance of the mixture was measured at each time interval.

The generation of 1O2 by different samples under laser irradiation was detected using a SOSG probe. Briefly, 2 mL of SOSG aqueous solution (5 μM) containing 1 mg mL−1 of samples were irradiated by a 650 nm laser at a power density of 0.15 W cm−2 for 14 min. The FL intensity of SOSG (excitation 505 nm) was measured at 2 min intervals using the Varioskan LUX Multimode Microplate Reader (Thermo Scientific).

To evaluate the catalytic ability of MnO2 on H2O2, the O2 generation performance of A@M/C-Mn was assessed by measuring the dissolved O2 concentration in aqueous solutions. Specifically, 100 μg mL−1 of A@M/C-Mn was introduced into 1 mM H2O2 solutions at different pH values (7.4, 6.5, and 5) and different temperatures (25 °C and 37 °C). The O2 concentration in these solutions was monitored using a portable dissolved O2 meter with an O2 electrode probe for 5 min at 20-second intervals. Additionally, the O2 generation of A@M/C-Mn at varying concentrations (0, 50, and 100 μg mL−1) in the presence of 1 mM H2O2 (pH 6.5) was measured using the same method. The consumption of H2O2 was also evaluated using an H2O2 detection kit according to the manufacturer's protocol. The O2 generation rate of A@M/C-Mn was compared with that of A@M/C under identical conditions (100 μg mL−1, pH 6.5, 1 mM H2O2).

The murine breast cancer cell line (4T1 cells) was cultured in RPMI 1640 medium (Gibco™) supplemented with 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin antibiotics. The cells were maintained at 37 °C in an incubator containing 5 % CO2. To investigate sample internalization, 4T1 cells (1 × 105 cells/well) were initially cultured in 35 mm glass-bottom dishes and incubated for 24 h. Samples of CDs, M/C, A@M/C, and A@M/C-Mn at equivalent concentrations of AIPH and CDs were then added. After incubation for a certain duration, the cells were stained with LysoTracker™ Green (50 nM) for 30 min and Hoechst 3344 (1 μg mL−1) for 10 min. The stained cells were subsequently imaged using CLSM with the following settings: green channel (excitation 488 nm/emission 510–540 nm), blue channel (excitation 365 nm/emission 450–530 nm) and red channel (excitation 420 nm/emission 550–700 nm).

To assess the cytotoxicity of each sample under various conditions, the standard MTS assay was conducted in both hypoxic (1 % O2) and normoxic (21 % O2) atmospheres. Specifically, 4T1 cells (1 × 105 cells/well) were seeded in a 96-well plate and allowed to adhere overnight in either normoxic or hypoxic conditions. Subsequently, the cells were treated with various samples at an equivalent concentration of AIPH and CDs for an additional 4 h and selectively irradiated by a 650 nm laser (L0.15:0.15 W cm−2, L1: 1 W cm−2) for 10 min before being washed with PBS. After overnight incubation, 20 μL of MTS was added for another 4 h of incubation. The absorbance at 492 nm was measured using the Varioskan LUX Multimode Microplate Reader (Thermo Scientific, USA). Cell viability was calculated using the following formula based on the ratio of OD values between the experimental groups (As), control group (Ac), and black wells (Ab):
Cell viability (%) = (As − Ab)/(Ac − Ab) × 100 %

Additionally, a live/dead cell assay was employed to visualize cell cytotoxicity using CLSM. 4T1 cells (1 × 105 cells/well) were initially cultured in 35 mm glass-bottomed dishes and incubated in a normoxic atmosphere for 12 h. After the addition of samples and allowing for cell uptake for 4 h, the cells were washed with PBS followed by laser irradiation for 10 min. Subsequently, the cells were stained with a live/dead cell staining kit according to the manufacturer's protocol and imaged using CLSM with excitation at 488 nm (live) and 570 nm (dead) immediately.

4T1 cells (1 × 105 cells/well) were seeded into 35 mm glass-bottom dishes. After treatment with different samples and irradiated by 650 nm laser, cells were stained with JC-1 (5 μM) at 37 °C for 20 min and observed by CLSM according to manufacturer’s report.

The intracellular free radicals, including
R and 1O2, were evaluated using DCFH-DA as an indicator. 4T1 cells (1 × 105 cells/well) were first seeded in 35 mm glass-bottomed dishes and incubated under either normoxic or hypoxic condition for 12 h. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with different samples containing equivalent concentrations of AIPH and CDs for 4 h. After rinsing twice with PBS, the cells were selectively treated with a laser for 10 min and then stained with 10 µM DCFH-DA for 30 min. The FL of the formed DCF, resulting from the reaction between free radicals and non-fluorescent DCFH-DA, was recorded using CLSM with an excitation setup at 488 nm.

All animal protocols followed the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of the Department of Health, Hong Kong SAR. Ethics approval was obtained from The Hong Kong Polytechnic University (Ref No. 23-24/801-BME-R-SZG), with an animal license issued by the Department of Health (Ref No. (23-96) in DH/HT&A/8/2/4Pt.13). Female Balb/c nude mice (4–5 weeks) were inoculated subcutaneously with 4T1 cells (1 × 106) in the upper thigh as tumor model. The mice were used when the tumor volume reached around 90–120 mm3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo imaging
ce:section.ce:para: For T1-weighted relaxivity and MR imaging, samples with different Mn concentrations were measured by 3T MR imaging instrument. Relaxivity values were obtained from the slope of the linear fitting of 1/T relaxation time (s−1) versus Mn concentration (mM). The T1-weighted images were acquired under the following parameters: TE = 17 ms, TR = 500 ms, ETL = 3, ENX = 8, matrix = 160 × 132, slice gap = 0.3 mm, and the slice thickness = 3 mm.

Besides, different concentrations of the A@M/C-Mn (0–1.5 mg mL−1) were injected into EP tube and then measured by Fujifilm visualsonics vevo LAZR muti-modality imaging platform (Amsterdam, NL). An integrated fiberoptic linear array LZ250 transducer with an axial resolution of 75 μm was used at a frequency of 21 MHz.

In vivo images were obtained at various time points following the post-injection of A@M/C-Mn (2 mg mL−1, 100 μL) into the tumor model. FL images were captured at 670 nm with excitation at 420 nm using the Perkin-Elmer IVIS Lumina Series III Pre-clinical equipment at 0, 1, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 24 h post-injection. Additionally, PA images were acquired in PA mode using the Fujifilm LAZR system with the LZ 250 transducer at time points of post-injection (0, 1, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 24 h). The signals in US mode and the measurement of sO2 were also recorded at each time point. Furthermore, in vivo MR imaging was conducted at 0, 1, 3, 6, and 9 h post-injection utilizing a 3T MR imaging instrument with the same parameters employed in vitro.

To quantitative assessment of the biodistribution of A@M/C-Mn, tumors and main organs were harvested at different time points after injection. Subsequently, they were digested in HNO3 for the quantification of Mn content using ICP-AES.

The tumor-bearing mice models were randomly classified into 9 groups (n = 3): (1) Blank, (2) Laser (L1), (3)A@M/C-Mn, (4) M/C + L0.15, (5) A@M/C + L0.15, (6) A@M/C-Mn + L0.15, (7) M/C + L1, (8) A@M/C + L1, and (9) A@M/C-Mn + L1. Laser irradiation at power density of 0.15 W cm−2 (L0.15) and 1 W cm−2 (L1) was locally administered to the tumor site for 10 min starting at 6 h post-injection. Temperature changes and IR images were captured using an IR thermal camera to demonstrate the in vivo photothermal performance during the procedure. Subsequently, tumor size and body weight of each group were monitored every 2 days for a total of 14 days. Tumor volume was calculated using the formula V = ab2/2, where a and b represent the length and width of the tumor, respectively. Following the treatment, all mice were euthanized, and the weight of the excised tumors was recorded.

Whole blood was collected into an anticoagulant tube and immediately centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 10 min. The red blood cells (RBCs) were isolated after washing with PBS and re-dispersed in PBS. Subsequently, A@M/C-Mn at various concentrations was added to the RBC solutions and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The absorbance of the supernatant at 540 nm following high-speed centrifugation was then measured. RBCs in DI H2O and PBS without the addition of the samples served as negative and positive controls, respectively.

The main organs at 14 days post-treatment and tumors at 1-day post-treatment were harvested and preserved in a 4 % paraformaldehyde solution (4 % PFA, ExCell Bio. Ltd.). Subsequently, all tissues were embedded in paraffin blocks, sectioned, and stained with H&E according to manufacturer’s protocol. Apoptotic cells in the tumor sections were determined using a TUNEL kit according to manufacturer’s protocol.

The synthesis process of A@M/C-Mn is illustrated in Fig. 1. NH2-functionalized MSN, with its inherent porous structure, was used as a nanocarrier, while CDs served as the photosensitizer. Both components were synthesized according to established procedures from previous studies [32,33]. TEM images revealed the morphologies of CDs and MSN, demonstrating sizes of approximately 3.5 nm and 50 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 A, B). Following the loading of AIPH into the MSN pores, negatively charged CDs were electrostatically attached to positively charged A@M, forming the A@M/C composite (Fig. 2C). Subsequently, in situ formation of MnO2 NS occurred through a redox reaction between KMnO4 and A@M/C, followed by PEGylation to enhance hydrophilicity, resulting in a stable A@M/C-Mn aqueous solution characterized by a yellow color (Fig. S1). TEM images of A@M/C-Mn in Fig. 2D displayed an average size of 60 nm, while fringes with a 0.24 nm spacing could be attributed to the δ-MnO2 structure. Elemental analysis via EDS and element mapping further confirmed the successful wrapping of MnO2 on A@M/C, with spatial overlap of Mn, O, Si, and N elements (Fig. 2E–G). As shown in Fig. S2, XPS analysis was performed to determine the elements in A@M/C-Mn. The full XPS spectrum of A@M/C-Mn verified the presence of Mn, along with other expected elements, including O, N, C, and Si. The characteristic peaks at ∼653.7 eV and ∼641.9 eV could be attributed to the binding energy of the Mn 2p in MnO2 [54]. Complementary structural characterization was conducted using XRD. As shown in Fig. S3, the XRD pattern revealed the crystalline structure of MnO2, predominantly corresponding to δ-MnO2 (PDF #18-0802). The characteristic peaks at 18.8°, 36.5°, and 66.0° were attributed to the (1 0 1), (0 0 6), and (1 1 9) reflections, respectively. Additionally, the presence of MSN was demonstrated by the diffraction peak around 23.2°. No distinct diffraction peaks for CDs were found because of their disordered structure and nanoscale size. Next, dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement revealed that the average hydrodynamic size of A@M/C-Mn was approximately 117 nm, slightly larger than the TEM result due to swelling in water (Fig. S4). Variations in surface charges among samples were also summarized in Fig. S5. The zeta potential of A@M/C shifted from +30 mV to +12 mV, indicating successful encapsulation of AIPH and integration of negatively charged CDs. After in situ growth of MnO2 NS and PEGylation, the zeta potential dropped to −2.5 mV, indicating the success of the encapsulation process. Collectively, minimal changes in hydrodynamic size and zeta potential observed over the 14-day storage period suggested that A@M/C-Mn possessed exceptional physiological stability (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. 2H, the characteristic peaks observed at 798 cm−1 and 1092 cm−1 were attributed to the vibrations of Si–OH bending and Si–O–Si stretching in the MSN, respectively. A comparison of the spectra of A@M/C and A@M/C-Mn with that of MSN revealed specific peaks associated with AIPH and CDs, such as the N–H, C–H, and C–O stretching peaks at 2834–2975 cm−1, 1598 cm−1, and 1640 cm−1, respectively, confirming the presence of encapsulated AIPH and electrostatically absorbed CDs. Furthermore, all specific peaks were also present in the spectrum of A@M/C-Mn, indicating that the wrapped MnO2 NS did not lead to structural alteration of A@M/C. The UV–Vis spectrum of A@M exhibited enhanced absorption in the 300–400 nm range compared to MSN, attributed to the effective loading of AIPH (Fig. 2I). The broad absorbance band in the NIR region indicated successful integration of CDs with A@M, highlighting the favorable photothermal performance of A@M/C. Following the wrapping of MnO2 NS, the A@M/C-Mn showed a surface plasma band with typical absorption peak around 300 nm. The loading content of AIPH was determined to be 16 % by calculating the difference in quantity between the initial solution and the supernatant after loading (Fig. S7). Furthermore, the loading content of CDs was measured to be 22 %, and the MnO2 content was quantified to be 12 % using ICP-AES analysis. Therefore, by integrating the synthesis details, the weight ratio could be calculated as AIPH: MSN: CDs: MnO2: PEG = 9.5 %: 59.7 %: 15.2 %: 12 %: 3.6 %. Interestingly, the emission of A@M/C-Mn exhibited significant quenching compared to A@M/C due to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) phenomenon between MnO2 and CDs (Fig. 2J) [46]. The calculated quenching efficiency was found to be 86 %, suggesting the potential for selective FL recovery in the TME. To quantitatively confirm this quenching effect, we measured the FL quantum yield and lifetime. As shown in Fig. S8, A@M/C-Mn displayed a significantly reduced FL lifetime of 0.7 ns and a quantum yield of 2.6 %, compared to the free CDs that had a lifetime of 4.5 ns and a quantum yield of 14 % [55]. This data further validated the efficient FL quenching in A@M/C-Mn, primarily due to FRET and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) interactions between CDs and MnO2 [56].

To evaluate the photothermal performance of the as-prepared nanoplatform, continuous laser irradiation at 650 nm with varying power densities was conducted on A@M/C-Mn dispersions at different concentrations (0–200 μg mL−1) for a duration of 10 min. Real-time temperature changes and IR thermal images were captured using a thermal camera. The temperature increase correlated positively with both the laser irradiation time and the sample concentration (Fig. 3 A). After 10 min of laser exposure, the temperature of the A@M/C-Mn dispersion at 100 μg mL−1 reached approximately 45 °C, which is sufficient to induce cancer cell death [57]. Similar photothermal performance curves were observed for A@M/C, whereas the A@M group showed negligible temperature increase (Fig. S9). Additionally, the impact of laser power density on the temperature change of A@M/C-Mn was assessed (Fig. 3B). At a lower power density (0.15 W cm−2), there was no significant heat generation in the A@M/C-Mn dispersion (100 μg mL−1), while increasing the power density to 1.5 W cm−2 raised the temperature to 55 °C. The corresponding IR images of A@M/C-Mn at various concentrations under 1 W cm−2 laser irradiation were collected in Fig. 3C. These findings indicated that the energy output from the 650 nm laser was effectively absorbed and converted into heat by A@M/C-Mn. Furthermore, four cycles of laser-on and laser-off processes were carried out to assess the photothermal stability of A@M/C-Mn. As illustrated in Fig. 3D, no significant attenuation was observed across the testing cycles, indicating the robust photostability of A@M/C-Mn.

Solid tumors often create a hypoxic (low O2 content) and acidic environment due to their rapid growth, abnormal vasculature, and altered metabolism. The inadequate blood supply to certain areas of the tumor leads to hypoxia, which in turn can promote the production of acidic metabolites such as lactic acid through anaerobic glycolysis [58]. Fortunately, the intrinsic catalytic activity of MnO2 NS facilitates the conversion of H2O2 to O2 within the acidic tumor milieu, thus promoting the efficacy of O2-dependent PDT. A portable dissolved O2 meter was employed to quantitatively assess the catalytic performance of A@M/C-Mn towards H2O2 at varying concentrations and across different pH levels. As illustrated in Fig. 3E, F, O2 gas bubbles were promptly generated upon the introduction of A@M/C-Mn into an acidic H2O2 solution (pH 6.5), and the amount of bubbles increased with the rise in A@M/C-Mn concentration and extended reaction time. As shown in Fig. S10, we investigated Mn2+ release from A@M/C-Mn at different pH values using ICP-AES. At pH 6.5, Mn2+ release reached approximately 89 % within 8 h, significantly higher than the ∼12 % release observed at pH 7.0. This pH-dependent release aligned with the catalytic O2 generation data, where the lower pH promoted Mn2+ release and enhanced the MnO2-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2. Additionally, TEM images revealed that the MnO2 layer progressively degraded under these conditions, further confirming the results (Fig. 3G–I). The rate of O2 generation by A@M/C-Mn increased with the acidity of the solution, primarily due to the enhanced protonation of H2O2 (Fig. 4A). Moreover, an elevated temperature of 37 °C resulted in a higher O2 generation rate compared to 25 °C (Fig. S11A), suggesting that photothermal effects may play a role in boosting O2 production. To validate this hypothesis, O2 generation was assessed both with and without laser irradiation. As shown in Fig. S11B, a significant increase in O2 production was observed under laser irradiation compared to the non-irradiated condition. These results directly demonstrated that photothermal effects effectively enhanced O2 generation, which was expected to improve PDT efficacy. In line with the enhanced O2 generation in acidic environments, the concentration of H2O2, measured using a H2O2 detection kit, decreased with increasing acidity, indicating accelerated consumption of H2O2 under acidic conditions (Fig. S12).

The generation of
R from AIPH decomposition was quantified using ABTS as an indicator, based on absorbance changes of produced ABTS+ during the reactions. As depicted in Fig. 4 B and Fig. S13, AIPH decomposition under thermal conditions was assessed at 25, 37, and 45 °C, respevtively. The absorbance of mixture between 500 and 900 nm at 45 °C was significantly higher in contrast to other temperature points with the same incubation time, indicating that elevated temperature accelerated the decomposition of AIPH, resulting in the generation of more
R.

Besides, no significant difference was observed in the ABTS+ production between hypoxic and normoxic conditions, revealing the O2-independent feature of
R formation (Fig. 4C). The release of
R from AIPH in different samples upon laser irradiation was also measured. As depicted in Fig. 4D, E, the absorbance of ABTS+ in the ABTS and A@M/C-Mn mixture increased with prolonged laser irradiation (650 nm, 1 W cm−2), showing significant differences compared to AIPH and M/C-Mn under identical conditions. To directly confirm the generation of
R, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was performed using 5,5-Dimethyl-1-Pyrroline-N-Oxide (DMPO) as a spin-trapping agent. As shown in Fig. S14A, the intensity of the characteristic peaks increased significantly with irradiation time, confirming a time-dependent generation of
R by A@M/C-Mn under acidic H2O2 conditions. Additionally, the impact of laser power density ranging from 0 to 1 W cm−2 on AIPH decomposition was evaluated (Fig. S15 and Fig. S16). Increasing laser power density resulted in higher temperatures that facilitated the rapid release of AIPH from mesoporous structures, accelerating
R generation. A comparison between the laser-off group and the 0.15 W cm−2 laser-on group showed no significant difference, suggesting that the thermal effect of such lower laser power on AIPH decomposition was negligible. The sustained release profiles of AIPH from A@M/C-Mn were assessed using the ABTS assay across varying pH values at 37 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 4F, the cumulative release of AIPH approached approximately 80 % after 36 h under acidic conditions. In contrast, minimal leakage of AIPH was observed at pH 7.4 over extended periods, highlighting the stable nature of A@M/C-Mn under normal physiological conditions and its pH-sensitive releasing behavior in the TME.

Given that the CDs-mediated PDT process consumes O2 to produce 1O2, SOSG was employed as an indicator to specifically evaluate the 1O2 generation in each group, both with and without laser irradiation (650 nm, 0.15 W cm−2), under normoxic and hypoxic conditions. As shown in Fig. S17, CDs with laser irradiation in normoxic environment exhibited increased FL intensity due to the O2-dependent 1O2 generation, while negligible changes were observed in the hypoxic group. Then, the 1O2 generation of A@M/C-Mn in different environment under normoxic condition was investigated. As shown in Fig. S18, A@M/C-Mn group displayed slight FL increase from SOSG, while higher FL increase was found in A@M/C group which implied 1O2 generation under laser irradiation. Notably, much more 1O2 could be generated by the A@M/C-Mn in acidic environment. The generation of 1O2 from A@M/C-Mn in the presence of acidic H2O2 was significantly higher compared to other groups, owing to the additional O2 supplied by MnO2-indcued H2O2 decomposition. This phenomenon was attributed to the MnO2 coating, which shielded the CDs and prevents the generation of 1O2 by redirecting the absorbed optical energy from the CDs to the MnO2 NS. Conversely, the gatekeeper MnO2 was degraded in response to acidic pH and H2O2 within the TME, subsequently activating CDs in A@M/C-Mn for FL recovery and suppling O2 for promoted 1O2 production (Fig. S19). To validate the SOSG findings and provide direct evidence of 1O2 generation, we conducted ESR measurements using 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone hydrochloride (TEMP) as a spin trap. As shown in Fig. S14B, the ESR spectra of A@M/C-Mn in acidic H2O2 under laser irradiation exhibited a time-dependent increase in the intensity of the characteristic 1O2 peaks. This provides compelling, direct evidence of 1O2 generation and corroborates the results obtained with SOSG.

Furthermore, the 1O2-generating abilities of A@M/C and A@M/C-Mn were investigated under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions. As shown in Fig. 4G, A@M/C exhibited negligible ability to generate 1O2, whereas A@M/C-Mn maintained its capacity for excellent 1O2 production, facilitated by acidic H2O2 and unaffected by hypoxia. This consistency suggested that A@M/C-Mn can overcome hypoxia through self-supplied O2, ensuring sustained 1O2 generation comparable to that in normoxic condition. Additionally, the impact of reaction time on the oxidation of SOSG was conducted at 2-min intervals (Fig. 4H). In the presence of acidic H2O2, the continuously increasing FL intensity of SOSG revealed ongoing 1O2 in the A@M/C-Mn group under laser irradiation, reaching a maximum at approximately 10 min. Meanwhile, the FL recovery rate of CDs in A@M/C-Mn at acidic or neutral pH conditions was investigated (Fig. 4I). The relative FL intensity (F/F0) indicated that 80 % of CDs were cumulatively activated under acidic environment (pH 6.5) within 24 h, whereas only 5 % were activated under neutral conditions. These results collectively demonstrated the pH/H2O2-responsive nature of A@M/C-Mn for enhanced 1O2 generation in tumor tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and in vitro free radicals generation
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the cellular uptake behavior, 4T1 breast cancer cells were incubated with A@M/C-Mn for varying durations (0, 2, 4, and 6 h). Cell nuclei and lysosomes were stained with Hoechst and LysoTracker, respectively, for blue and green FL, enabling co-localization with the red FL emitted by CDs. As depicted in Fig. S20 and Fig. S21, the intensity of red FL gradually increased with prolonged incubation times, and it was observed to localize within the lysosomes and cytoplasm, suggesting efficient internalization of A@M/C-Mn and controllable FL recovery. Additionally, FL signal comparisons were made between CDs, A@M/C, and A@M/C-Mn after 4 h of incubation (Fig. 5 A). The red FL observed in the A@M/C-Mn treated group was slightly weaker than that in the other groups, indicating successful decomposition of MnO2 NS and activation of CDs in 4T1 cells.

The production of intracellular free radicals, including 1O2 and
R, was evaluated in 4T1 breast cancer cells under laser irradiation. Considering that the CDs-mediated PDT process consumes O2, while AIPH-mediated PDT is O2-independent, total free radicals production was detected using the DCFH-DA probe under varying O2 levels. In a normoxic environment (Fig. 5B), slight FL was observed in the blank, AIPH + L1, and A@M/C-Mn groups, indicating minimal free radicals levels. However, groups incubated with M/C, A@M/C, or A@M/C-Mn and subjected to laser irradiation exhibited significantly brighter green FL. The CDs in the M/C group demonstrated similar ROS generation capabilities under both low (L0.15: 0.15 W cm−2) and high (L1: 1 W cm−2) power density laser irradiation. As for the A@M/C group, heat generated by CDs under high power density laser irradiation (L1) facilitated the decomposition of AIPH, producing additional
R and enhancing DCFH-DA oxidation, resulting in much stronger green FL. More importantly, the A@M/C-Mn group showed a notable increase in free radicals levels due to the ample O2 supply from endogenous H2O2 in the presence of MnO2 NS. Additionally, intracellular free radicals levels were assessed under both hypoxic and normoxic conditions with laser irradiation (L1). As shown in Fig. S22, no FL was observed in the A@M−Mn group, confirming that laser irradiation and O2 supply did not generate free radicals from AIPH in the absence of CDs-mediated photothermal effects. M/C generated moderate free radicals in normoxia, while it produced minimal free radicals under hypoxia. The A@M/C group enhanced free radical production under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions compared to M/C, owing to the O2-independent nature of
R generation from AIPH. Similarly, M/C-Mn enhanced free radical formation under both conditions compared to M/C due to the additional O2 supply. Ultimately, the A@M/C-Mn group exhibited the brightest green FL under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions among all groups. These findings suggest that A@M/C-Mn can effectively address tumor hypoxia, providing a controlled and effective oxidative damage therapy through combined free radicals production.

To evaluate the in vitro anticancer efficacy, a standard MTS assay was performed on 4T1 breast cancer cells under various conditions. As shown in Fig. 5C, treatments with L1 or AIPH + L1 did not reduce the cell viability, and only slight cytotoxicity (<8 %) was observed in the A@M/C-Mn treatment group even at concentrations up to 100 μg mL−1, indicating its low dark cytotoxicity. Subsequently, the laser-triggered cytotoxicity of different samples against 4T1 cells were assessed in a powder density-dependent manner. Under normoxic conditions, M/C and A@M/C exhibited similar cytotoxic effects under lower power density laser irradiation (0.15 W cm−2), attributed to single-modal PDT induced by CDs. However, when the laser power density was increased to 1 W cm−2, the combined PTT and PDT effect of CDs resulted in significantly decreased cell viability compared to the M/C treated group. In the A@M/C-Mn group, a further enhancement in cytotoxicity was observed under the same conditions as A@M/C due to the presence of MnO2. The self-oxygenation from endogenous H2O2 facilitated the generation of 1O2, leading to the lowest survival rate owing to the synergistic effects of enhanced O2-dependent PDT, PTT and O2-independent PDT. Meanwhile, incubation of A@M/C-Mn with HUVEC or 3T3 cells showed negligible cytotoxicity, likely due to the relatively low H2O2 levels and neutral pH in normal cells (Fig. S23). The hemolysis rate remained minimal (2.1 %) even at a high concentration of 200 μg mL−1, demonstrating the excellent hemocompatibility of A@M/C-Mn (Fig. S24). All these results verified the outstanding biosafety of A@M/C-Mn for potential in vivo applications.

Apoptosis of 4T1 cells following various treatments was assessed via flow cytometry. As shown in Fig. S25, treatments with Blank or L1 alone had negligible impact on 4T1 cells apoptosis. However, increased apoptotic ratios in the other groups confirmed their antitumor effectiveness. Under laser irradiation at either 0.15 W cm−2 or 1 W cm−2, the A@M/C-Mn group showed an apoptotic ratio of 87.9 % and 42.8 %, respectively. These ratios surpassed those of the A@M/C (74.5 % and 23.9 %) and M/C (55.1 % and 17.2 %) groups, indicating the synergistic effects of O2-dependent and O2-independent PDT. Additionally, the apoptotic ratios in the M/C + L1, A@M/C + L1, and A@M/C-Mn + L1 groups increased significantly compared to those with lower power density (0.15 W cm−2), verifying the additional antitumor effects of PTT and O2-independent PDT. Mitochondrial membrane potential, a key indicator of mitochondrial dysfunction associated with apoptosis, was assessed using the JC-1 assay. Consistent with the previous results, 4T1 cells treated with A@M/C-Mn + L1 exhibited a significantly reduced red/green FL intensity ratio compared to the other groups, indicating the most pronounced mitochondrial dysfunction (Fig. S26). Furthermore, a distinguished live/dead cell staining was carried out using a LIVE/DEAD™ Cell Imaging Kit. As depicted in Fig. 5D, nearly all 4T1 cells in the A@M/C-Mn + L1 treated group exhibited intense red FL, indicative of remarkable cell-killing effects and therapeutic efficacy, further confirming the excellent curative outcome of enhanced synergistic PDT and PTT.

Given the promising cellular uptake and anticancer efficacy of A@M/C-Mn in vitro, we proceeded with in vivo experiments to evaluate its therapeutic potential. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, B, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were imaged using an IVIS system at various time points (0, 1, 3, 6, 9, 12 and 24 h) post-injection of A@M/C-Mn via the tail vein. The FL signal progressively accumulated at the tumor site, peaking around 6 h due to the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. These results indicated effective accumulation of A@M/C-Mn and specific recovery of FL within the tumor site. To further confirm the accumulation, a FUJI multi-mode imaging system was employed. Prior to in vivo testing, a dose-dependent PA signal was measured in a test tube, revealing a desirable linear relationship between PA signal intensities and A@M/C-Mn concentration, which underscored the potential of A@M/C-Mn as an ideal PA imaging contrast agent (Fig. 6C). Subsequently, the in vivo PA signals post-intravenous injection exhibited a similar trend to the FL imaging results, showing maximum contrast at 6 h (Fig. 6D–G). Concurrently measured US signals followed a comparable pattern, peaking at 6 h, which could be attributed to the increase in O2 bubbles produced by the decomposition of H2O2 in the TME. Blood oxygen saturation (sO2) signals also showed an increasing trend after intravenous injection, with a maximum signal at 6 h, aligning with the US signals due to the generation of O2 bubbles. This rise in sO2 indicated that A@M/C-Mn effectively ameliorated tumor hypoxia. These findings highlight the specific response of A@M/C-Mn at the tumor site, identifying 6 h post-injection as the optimal time point for maximum accumulation and therapeutic efficacy. Furthermore, in vitro and in vivo MR imaging were conducted to further assess the ability of A@M/C-Mn as an ideal contrast agent (Fig. 6H). In the presence of H2O2, A@M/C-Mn demonstrated significantly enhanced T1-weighted MR signal, with an r1 value of 10.38 mM−1 s−1 under acidic conditions compared to 1.07 mM−1 s−1 under neutral conditions without H2O2. This enhancement could be attributed to the faster decomposition of MnO2 NS to Mn2+ in acidic H2O2 solution. Subsequently, the maximum retention time of Mn2+ within the tumor was evaluated in the 4T1 tumor model (Fig. 6I). The T1 signal at the tumor site progressively increased over time and began to exhibit a decline after 6 h post-injection, consistent with the findings from FL/PA/US imaging (Fig. 6J). These results provided further confirmation of the accumulation process of A@M/C-Mn and the subsequent release of Mn2+ within the TME. Then the biodistribution of A@M/C-Mn in major organs and tumors was assessed through quantitative analysis of Mn ions using ICP-AES. The accumulation of A@M/C-Mn at tumor sites reached approximately 8.1 % ID/g at 6 h post-injection and was then gradually excreted via the kidneys (Fig. S27). To further elucidate the metabolic behavior of A@M/C-Mn within the TME over time, an extended analysis was conducted, revealing a gradual clearance of Mn ions from the tumor site at 6, 24, 48, and 72 h (Fig. S28). These findings strongly highlight the effectiveness of multi-modal FL/MR/PA/US imaging as a powerful tool for accurately monitoring A@M/C-Mn delivery kinetics, demonstrating significant potential for in vivo therapeutic applications.

To further assess the in vivo antitumor efficacy of A@M/C-Mn, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 9 groups (n = 3): (1) Blank, (2) Laser (L1), (3) A@M/C-Mn, (4) M/C + L0.15, (5) A@M/C + L0.15, (6) A@M/C-Mn + L0.15, (7) M/C + L1, (8) A@M/C + L1, and (9) A@M/C-Mn + L1. Mice in each group were intravenously injected with different samples and followed by 10 min of laser irradiation (650 nm) at 6 h post-injection for the phototherapy-involved groups. The temperature variation at the tumor site in laser treatment groups was monitored in real time by thermal camera. As shown in Fig. 7 A, B, the temperature of tumors in group M/C + L1, A@M/C + L1, and A@M/C-Mn + L1 increased to above 50 °C under 1 W cm−2 laser irradiation, which could induce hyperthermia and trigger the AIPH decomposition. In contrast, no significant increase in temperature was detected in laser only and lower power density treated groups, indicating the minimal photothermal conversion. Subsequent measurement of tumor volume and body weights every 2 days showed no significant weight loss in any group, indicating low systemic toxicity during the treatments (Fig. 7C).

The antitumor efficacy assessment revealed minimal tumor elimination in the Blank, Laser, and A@M/C-Mn groups, confirming the biosafety of laser and our nanoplatform (Fig. 7D). The groups of M/C + L0.15 and A@M/C + L0.15 irradiated at lower power density of 0.15 W cm−2 showed partial tumor growth inhibition due to the poor PDT effect under hypoxic TME, while the A@M/C-Mn group exhibited improved antitumor performance because of the self-supplied O2. Compared with lower power density treatment groups, the tumor growth inhibition rates were improved in groups of M/C + L1, A@M/C + L1, and A@M/C-Mn + L1, which could be contributed to additional PTT and enhanced PDT under 1 W cm−2 irradiation. Notably, the optimal tumor elimination effect in A@M/C-Mn + L1 group was owing to the combination of hyperthermia triggered PTT/O2-independent PDT and O2 self-supplying enhanced PDT, indicating the amelioration of tumor hypoxia post A@M/C-Mn treatment. Photographs of mice after 14 days of treatment, as well as the weights of harvested tumors, corroborated the results of tumor growth inhibition (Fig. 7E, F). Building upon these macroscopic observations and to delve into the cellular mechanisms, tumor sections were stained with TUNEL assay and H&E staining (Fig. 7G, H). TUNEL staining revealed a significant increase in apoptotic cells (positive staining) specifically in the A@M/C-Mn + L1 treated group compared with other groups, highlighting the potent apoptosis-inducing ability of the combination therapy (Fig. 7G). H&E staining further corroborated these findings, showing extensive tumor cell damage in the A@M/C-Mn + L1 group relative to the other groups (Fig. 7H). Collectively, the results from both TUNEL and H&E staining confirmed the exceptional therapeutic effect of our nanoplatform, which was primarily mediated through apoptosis. Furthermore, blood chemistry analyses assessing liver function (as indicated by aspartate aminotransferase, AST, and alanine aminotransferase, ALT) and kidney function (as indicated by creatinine, CREA, and Urea nitrogen, BUN) were conducted after 14 days of treatment. The results from A@M/C-Mn + L1 treated group showed no abnormities when compared to Blank group, revealing that insignificant renal or hepatic cytotoxicity was induced in vivo (Fig. S29). Histological analysis of main organ sections after treatment revealed no apparent changes, further supporting the absence of significant side effects throughout the treatment process (Fig. S30). Collectively, these results demonstrated the superior antitumor performance of A@M/C-Mn through co-enhanced PDT/PTT facilitated by O2 supply and
R generation.

In summary, this study successfully developed a novel intelligent nanoplatform, A@M/C-Mn, that integrates AIPH-loaded MSN, red CDs, and MnO2 NS for synergistic phototherapy and tetra-modal imaging. Key findings demonstrated that the MnO2 shell effectively responded to the acidic and H2O2-rich TME, triggering in situ O2 generation, Mn2+ release, and activation of encapsulated AIPH and CDs. This TME-triggered response addresses the hypoxia challenge commonly encountered in solid tumors, which is a major limitation of traditional O2-dependent PDT. Notably, the nanoplatform achieves a synergistic therapeutic effect through a dual-PDT mechanism: enhanced O2-dependent PDT via CDs, fueled by self-supplied O2 from endogenous H2O2 decomposition, and O2-independent PDT through the generation of
R radicals from PTT-induced AIPH disintegration. This unique combination overcomes PDT resistance in hypoxic tumors, representing a significant advancement over conventional PDT agents [6,59]. Furthermore, A@M/C-Mn operates via sequential TME and NIR laser irradiation activation, ensuring its excellent biosecurity. While previous studies have explored combined PDT/PTT, they mainly focused on therapeutic outcomes [57,60]. Our system, however, uniquely integrates TME-activated turn-on imaging for comprehensive theranostic monitoring. The multi-imaging capability, enabled by the intrinsic properties of CDs, Mn2+ release, and O2 generation, facilitates real-time tracking of nanoplatform biodistribution and therapeutic response [61,62]. Looking ahead, A@M/C-Mn holds substantial translational promise as a versatile theranostic agent for hypoxic tumors. Future work will focus on optimizing the nanoplatform to enhance tumor targeting specificity, improve tissue penetration, and explore its efficacy in orthotopic tumor models, as well as its potential synergy with other therapeutic strategies such as immunotherapy.
